name: Antigone
shortDescription: Antigone defies Creon's decree, burying her brother Polynices and facing the consequences of divine law versus human law.
description: Antigone is a central figure in Greek tragedy, primarily known through Sophocles' play, *Antigone*. She is the daughter of Oedipus and Jocasta, and her story revolves around her unwavering commitment to divine law and familial duty in the face of human decree. After Oedipus's death, her brothers, Eteocles and Polynices, fight for the throne of Thebes, resulting in both of their deaths. Creon, the new ruler, decrees that Eteocles, who defended Thebes, should be honored with a proper burial, while Polynices, who attacked the city, should be left unburied, a disgrace punishable by death. Antigone, however, defies Creon's edict, believing that the laws of the gods demand that all dead receive proper burial rites. Her act of defiance leads to her tragic downfall, highlighting themes of justice, morality, and the conflict between individual conscience and state authority. Antigone's story continues to resonate as a powerful exploration of moral courage and the consequences of standing up for one's beliefs.
subtitle: A tragedy of duty, defiance, and death.
author: Sophocles
significance: Explores the timeless conflict between individual conscience and state authority.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Antigone's origins are deeply intertwined with the cursed House of Oedipus. She is the daughter of Oedipus and Jocasta, making her both sister and daughter to Oedipus, due to the tragic circumstances of his unknowingly marrying his mother. The curse upon their family shapes her destiny. After Oedipus's death and the fratricidal conflict between her brothers, Antigone is faced with Creon's decree. Driven by a sense of familial duty and religious piety, she chooses to bury her brother Polynices, defying Creon's explicit command. This act sets in motion the tragic events of Sophocles' play, where Antigone confronts Creon, defends her actions, and ultimately accepts her fate. Her unwavering belief in the sanctity of burial rites and the importance of honoring the dead is central to her character and the mythology surrounding her.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Antigone's role in Greek tragedy is that of a moral exemplar, a figure who embodies the conflict between individual conscience and the demands of the state. She represents the power of personal conviction and the courage to stand up for what one believes is right, even in the face of death. Her defiance of Creon's decree challenges the authority of the state and raises questions about the limits of human law. Antigone's unwavering commitment to divine law and familial duty highlights the importance of respecting religious traditions and honoring the dead. Her tragic fate serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of tyranny and the consequences of suppressing individual freedom. She represents the voice of conscience against the overreach of political power.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Antigone is a powerful symbol of resistance against unjust authority. Her act of burying Polynices symbolizes the importance of upholding moral principles, even when they conflict with the laws of the state. She represents the individual's right to conscience and the need to challenge oppressive regimes. Antigone's unwavering commitment to divine law can be interpreted as a rejection of human arrogance and a recognition of the limits of human power. Her tragic death can be seen as a sacrifice for the sake of justice and a testament to the enduring power of moral courage. The play also explores the symbolism of burial rites, highlighting the importance of honoring the dead and ensuring their passage to the afterlife. Antigone's actions underscore the significance of these rituals and the consequences of denying them.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Antigone's story has had a profound and lasting influence on literature, philosophy, and political thought. Her character has been reinterpreted and reimagined in countless works of art, literature, and drama throughout history. Philosophers like Hegel have analyzed her story as a classic example of the conflict between individual conscience and the demands of the state. Playwrights such as Bertolt Brecht have adapted *Antigone* to address contemporary political issues. Her story continues to inspire activists and advocates for civil disobedience around the world. Antigone remains a powerful symbol of resistance against injustice and a reminder of the importance of upholding moral principles, even in the face of adversity. Her legacy endures as a testament to the enduring power of human courage and the timeless relevance of Greek tragedy.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: The primary source for the myth of Antigone, detailing her defiance of Creon's decree.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Provides the background for the Trojan War and some family history.

associations.name: Justice
associations.significance: Antigone's actions are driven by her belief in justice and divine law.

associations.name: Duty
associations.significance: Her actions are motivated by a sense of familial and religious duty.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Joan of Arc
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like Antigone, she defied earthly authority (the English crown) based on divine conviction, facing death for her beliefs.

companions.name: Creon
companions.description: The King of Thebes who forbids the burial of Polynices.

companions.name: Ismene
companions.description: Antigone's sister, who initially refuses to help bury Polynices.

companions.name: Haemon
companions.description: Antigone's fiancé and Creon's son, who defends Antigone's actions.

cultural.modernLegacy: Antigone's story continues to be adapted and reinterpreted in modern literature, theater, and film, serving as a powerful symbol of resistance and moral courage. The play is frequently studied in academic settings to explore themes of justice, law, and individual conscience.
